name: Bajji
description: Bajji is a popular South Indian tea-time snack where vegetables are dipped in a spiced gram flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp. Common vegetables used include raw banana, potato, onion rings, chilli bajji and brinjal. The batter is light, crispy and mildly spiced — perfect for rainy evenings with a hot cup of tea or coffee.

steps.instruction: Sift gram flour and rice flour together into a large bowl.
Add red chilli powder, asafoetida, baking soda, ginger powder, garlic powder and salt.
Add water little by little to make a smooth, thick batter. The consistency should coat the back of a spoon.
steps.tip: The batter should be thick enough to coat the vegetables well — too thin and it won't stick; too thick and it will be doughy.

steps.instruction: Heat oil in a kadai on medium heat.
Slice the vegetables into thin, even pieces. For chilli bajji, make a slit and remove seeds.
steps.tip: Slice vegetables evenly so they cook uniformly.

steps.instruction: Dip each vegetable slice into the batter, coating it completely.
Gently drop the coated vegetable into the hot oil. Fry a few at a time — do not overcrowd.
Fry on medium heat, turning occasionally, until golden and crisp on all sides.
steps.tip: For extra crispy bajji, add 1 tbsp hot oil to the batter before frying.

steps.instruction: Drain on paper towels. Serve hot with coconut chutney or tomato chutney.
steps.tip: Serve immediately while hot for the best crispiness.

tips: The batter should be thick enough to coat the vegetables well — too thin and it won't stick; too thick and it will be doughy.
Slice vegetables evenly so they cook uniformly.
For extra crispy bajji, add 1 tbsp hot oil to the batter before frying.
Adding rice flour to the batter makes the bajji extra crispy.
Fresh ginger-garlic paste can be added to the batter instead of powders for better flavour and to prevent gastric issues.
Serve immediately while hot for the best crispiness.
